content: I'm really torn about whether to expand my wedding planning business or focus on this new online course I'm developing. It's been keeping me up at night.
That sounds like a difficult decision. What aspects of each option appeal to you most?

Well, the wedding planning agency is doing well - I've got about 15 regular clients and we're booked solid for the next 8 months. But creating this course about destination wedding planning could really scale my expertise.
That's impressive growth! Have you considered how the course might complement your existing business rather than compete with it?
I have, actually. I was thinking I could film the course modules during my slow season - January through March. That's when I usually go surfing in Costa Rica anyway.
Filming in Costa Rica sounds like a great way to combine work and pleasure! How long have you been running your agency?
This is my 7th year. I started it right after my own destination wedding in Bali - I realized there was a real gap in the market for planners who understood the logistics of international weddings.
That's a great origin story! What's been the biggest challenge you've faced growing the business?
Honestly, finding reliable vendors in different countries. I once had a florist in Mexico deliver the wrong flowers and I had to knit replacement bouquets the night before the wedding!
You knit replacement bouquets? That's incredible! How did you learn to knit?
My grandmother taught me when I was 10. It's actually become a stress-relief hobby - I knit during conference calls and while watching Netflix. I've made over 50 scarves in the last year alone!
That's impressive dedication! Do you think your knitting skills have helped with wedding planning in unexpected ways?

Definitely. The patience and attention to detail transfer perfectly. Plus, I've incorporated knitting into several weddings - couples love the handmade touch. I even had a couple ask for knitted boutonnieres last spring.
That's such a unique offering! How do you balance all your interests - the business, the course, surfing, knitting, and travel blogging?

It's a constant juggling act. I usually dedicate mornings to client work, afternoons to the course development, and evenings to blogging. I surf whenever there's a good swell - I've got a tide app that's basically my second alarm clock.
You've really optimized your schedule! Have you thought about how the online course might change your daily routine?
I have. I'm thinking I'll need to hire an assistant to handle day-to-day client communications so I can focus on filming and editing. Maybe someone part-time, 20 hours a week to start.
That sounds like a smart move. What's holding you back from making the decision?

The financial risk mainly. The course will cost about $8,000 to produce properly - good cameras, editing software, maybe even a small studio space. But I keep thinking about how I could reach couples planning weddings in places I've never been.
That's a significant investment, but it sounds like you've thought it through carefully. What would your course cover specifically?

Everything from choosing international venues to navigating foreign marriage laws, plus budget planning and vendor coordination across time zones. I want to share all the hard lessons I've learned so couples don't have to learn them the expensive way like I did.
